text: inch. He’d prove as immovable as a mountain. But his stare was heavy, and she’d been aware of it throughout . In the end, he hadn’t even enjoyed it. Her reaction, naturally, had been anger . So why had she dreamt of him? Why was she so aroused, when she should reject the very idea of his touch ? Because he was different . “Dammit, no!” She dropped onto the edge of the bed, pressing her fists into her thighs, and groaned . “ No .” She needed to forget about this. There was no telling how long he’d maintain interest in her, but she would have to dance for him if she wanted food and shelter. If she wanted him to help find Tabitha. But the only way to turn her thoughts away from Ronin was to dwell on the darkness of her past…and she refused to do that, even after she’d been betrayed by her body and her dreams . Swinging her legs onto the bed, she grabbed the wadded blanket and shook it over herself as she lay back down, tucking it under her backside so she didn’t feel the wet spot on the sheets. She didn’t need a reminder of her weakness . Lara closed her eyes. Time passed, and sleep refused to come . Creaking footsteps overhead jarred her. She stared up at the shadowed ceiling and listened to him move around, surprisingly quiet for how heavy he was. Why was he back up there? Was he as hungry for answers as Lara was? It was obvious that he’d never gone up into that room; everything within had been undisturbed, coated with years-worth of dust . She was tempted to pull on some pants and go up there with him, but the phantom of her dream swept over her mind. She couldn’t be around him right now. Not while she was uncertain of how she’d react . Not while the feel of him inside her was fresh in her memory . * * * R onin stood with his hands clasped behind his back, staring at the journal atop the chest. It lay amongst his tools, standing out from them despite being just as old and worn. The electrodes in his fingertips fired off, sending a twitch through his fingers . Just a glitch . He’d been too long in the Dust on his last run, had endured more wear and tear than normal. That was the reason for the minor, fleeting malfunction in his hand. It was not because his desire to pick up the book and read was warring with his concern for what he’d discover inside . The journal couldn’t do any harm. It was just ink on paper. All the words in the world, all the thoughts, could accomplish nothing without action. And whatever actions were described on those pages had concluded many years ago . He stepped up to the chest and reached down, lifting the book from its place. Paper and ink. Its weight was barely enough to register to him, as airy and meaningless as the information it held . If only he believed that . Holding the journal on his palm, he opened it to the first page . Those things killed people today. Marched into town and just started killing people. I watched from the window as people were dragged into the park and executed . The last time Ronin had looked over the park, it was peaceful, bathed in evening sunlight. Trees and grass swayed in the breeze, and glittering ripples skittered across the surface of the pond . Though he could have read the page entirely in the time it took to glance at it, he forced himself to slow down, to analyze each word . It doesn’t look like they’re going house-to-house yet, which is probably the only reason I’m still here to write this. Pretty sure the Joneses and the Ortegas were down there. I didn’t notice anyone else that looked familiar, but I wasn’t about to press my face to the glass and show them where I’m hiding . Night’s already falling, but everything is bright across the street. The fire’s still burning. I’m glad the wind is moving west, or I’d have to smell them . The page ended there, with the bottom half blank. Ronin turned to the next . Found out from Mandy Weiss earlier this morning that the shit show in the park was because THEY wanted part of the city cleared out. Said it was THEIR right to inhabit that part of Cheyenne, as it’s their kind that’s kept it running. People here have been scared for years – since well before the damned world ended – and there’s nothing beyond the city but dust and radiation. Where the hell were those people supposed to go ? She told me the leader of the robots gave the humans the day to pack up and leave their homes. Most stayed. When 24 hours passed – ex-fucking-zactly 24 hours, down to the second – those tin bastards started going into the buildings and dragging people out . It’s only a matter of time before they start clearing out more of these homes. I’ll have to keep my ear to the ground . Ronin’s brow furrowed. He’d been to places where humans and bots lived apart from one another, but the separation usually seemed a matter of comfort and practicality. Flesh and bone had different needs than metal and electronics. He couldn’t recall hearing of such open, widescale hostility between the two before . At least the entry gave him a probable time frame. People here have been scared for years — since well before the damned world ended . That had to be referring to the Black Out . Realized I haven’t been dating this. Guess it doesn’t really matter, though. Probably won’t be anyone left to read this in a decade or two, at the rate we’re
